title: New, Orange-Lipped Monkey Discovered in Africa
gist: A Florida Atlantic University-led study formally identified a new orange-lipped colobus monkey species, Colobus congoensis (locally known as 'Likweli'), from the east-central Democratic Republic of the Congo, using genetic, anatomical, and acoustic analysis — one of only a handful of new African monkey species named in 75 years.

insights.claim: A study led by Florida Atlantic University researchers identified a new primate species from the east-central Democratic Republic of the Congo — an orange-lipped colobus monkey named Colobus congoensis — making it one of only a handful of new monkey species identified in Africa in the last 75 years.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Formal species discoveries in well-studied, large-bodied taxa like primates are rare (roughly once in decades per region), underscoring how much biodiversity in remote habitats like the Congo basin remains scientifically undocumented.
insights.locator: a study led by Florida Atlantic University researchers

insights.claim: Researchers identified Colobus congoensis using a combination of genetic, anatomical, and acoustic analyses, rather than visual observation alone.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Shows the multi-modal (genetic + morphological + bioacoustic) methodology now standard for formally validating new species claims.

insights.claim: Local Congo basin communities were already aware of the new colobus monkey before its scientific description — the Balanga community calls it 'Likweli' and the Mituku community calls it 'Kasaba Nkoni' ('branch shaker'), though limited scientific documentation existed because of the species' elusive habits.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Highlights how indigenous ecological knowledge frequently predates and can accelerate formal scientific species descriptions in under-surveyed regions.
insights.locator: inhabitants of the remote Congo basin were aware of the primate
